title: Build Boundary Hygiene
use_for: ensuring source archives, generated files, build outputs, and tests are included or excluded deliberately
preventing legacy or scratch code from entering compilation or packaging
reviewing project files and wildcard rules during a rewrite
keeping runtime, tests, tools, resources, and archives separated
do_not_use_for: package manager upgrades as the primary task
rules: compile, package, test, and generated-file boundaries must be explicit
archive and build-output directories must not be included by broad wildcards
test code should not ship unless the project intentionally embeds tests
generated files should have a known owner and regeneration path
build exclusions should be verified, not assumed
procedure: Identify source, archive, tests, tools, resources, generated files, and build outputs.
Inspect project include and exclude rules for wildcard leaks.
Verify archive and output directories are absent from compile and package inputs.
Verify tests are discovered only by test projects or intended test runners.
Record any intentional exceptions with their owner and reason.
Run a build, dry-run, or file listing check to prove the boundary.

primary_when: the task is primarily about what enters compilation, packaging, tests, or generated outputs
must_not_own_when: the task is primarily about domain modeling or runtime module design

solves: how to keep archived and generated code out of unintended build surfaces
how to make project-file boundaries reviewable during rewrites
how to avoid shipping or compiling stale code by accident

core_principles: Wildcard defaults are useful only when exclusions are reviewed.
A rewrite archive is not safe until the build proves it is excluded.
Packaging and test discovery can leak code even when compilation does not.
constraints: Do not assume folder moves change build behavior.
Do not encode local absolute paths in reusable guidance.
Do not let build outputs or scratch files participate in source compilation.
Do not hide build exceptions without an owner and reason.
decision_rules: If a directory contains legacy or scratch code, exclude it explicitly.
If a directory contains generated code, document whether it is source input or output.
If tests live near runtime code, verify runtime packages cannot include them.
If compile item rules are broad, search for negative patterns and generated-output leaks.

preferred_patterns: separate runtime, test, tool, generated, archive, and output boundaries
verification by build input listing or actual build
avoid_patterns: archived code still visible to tests or packaging
absolute local paths in reusable build rules

recipes.name: Exclude an archive from build surfaces
recipes.when: Use when old code is kept for reference during a rewrite.
steps: Add explicit compile, package, test, and generation exclusions for the archive boundary.
Check wildcard include rules for leaks.
Run a build or source item listing to verify exclusion.
Search for runtime references to archived symbols.
Record the archive boundary rule in rewrite notes.

pitfalls: Excluding runtime but not tests or generators.
Leaving temporary files under source wildcards.
Using absolute machine paths as reusable policy.
review_checklist: Are archive, output, generated, test, and tool boundaries explicit?
Is reusable guidance free of local paths and project names?

references.title: Bootstrap
references.interpretation: Use when build boundaries affect runtime entry and startup composition.
references.why: Build inputs determine what the runtime can accidentally discover or execute.
